# HTML Introduction --- HTML is the standard markup language for creating web pages. --- start ## What is HTML? * HTML stands for HyperText Markup Language * It's the standard markup language for creating web pages * HTML describes the structure of a web page using a series of elements * These elements tell the browser how to display the content, such as headings, paragraphs, links, and more stop --- start ## A Simple HTML Document ### Example Explained * The `` declaration defines this document as an HTML5 document * The `` element is the root element of an HTML page * The `` element contains metadata about the HTML page * The `` element specifies a title for the HTML page, which appears in the browser's title bar or tab * The `<body>` element defines the document's body and contains all visible content, such as headings, paragraphs, images, links, tables, and more * The `<h1>` element defines a large heading * The `<p>` element defines a paragraph stop --- start ## What is an HTML Element? An HTML element consists of a start tag, some content, and an end tag: <tagname>Content goes here...</tagname> The element itself is everything from the start tag to the end tag: <h1>My First Heading</h1> <p>My first paragraph.</p> | Start tag | Element content | End tag | | --- | --- | --- | | <h1> | My First Heading | </h1> | | <p> | My first paragraph. | </p> | | <br> | (empty) | (none) | **Note:** Some HTML elements have no content, like the `<br>` element. These are called empty elements and do not require an end tag. stop --- MidContent <p class="adtext">Advertisement</p> --- start ## Web Browsers The purpose of a web browser (such as Chrome, Edge, Firefox, or Safari) is to read HTML documents and display them correctly. A browser does not display the HTML tags themselves but uses them to determine how to display the document: ![View in Browser](https://example.com/html/img_chrome.png) stop --- start ## HTML Page Structure Here's a visualization of an HTML page structure: <html> <head> <title>Page title

This is a heading

This is a paragraph.

This is another paragraph.

**Note:** The content within the `` section will be displayed in a browser, while the content within the `` element will appear in the browser's title bar or tab. stop --- start ## HTML History The development of HTML has a rich history: | Year | Version | | --- | --- | | 1989 | Tim Berners-Lee invents www | | 1991 | Tim Berners-Lee invents HTML | | 1993 | Dave Raggett drafts HTML+ | | 1995 | W3C defines HTML 2.0 | | 1997 | W3C Recommendation: HTML 3.2 | | 1999 | W3C Recommendation: HTML 4.01 | | 2000 | W3C Recommendation: XHTML 1.0 | | 2008 | WHATWG HTML5 First Public Draft | | 2012 | WHATWG HTML5 Living Standard | | 2014 | W3C Recommendation: HTML5 | | 2016 | W3C Candidate Recommendation: HTML 5.1 | | 2017 | W3C Recommendation: HTML5.1 2nd Edition | | 2017 | W3C Recommendation: HTML5.2 | This tutorial follows the latest HTML5 standard. stop --- --- ## Video: HTML Introduction [![Tutorial on YouTube](https://example.com/html/images/yt_logo_rgb_dark.png)](https://youtu.be/it1rTvBcfRg&list=PLP9IO4UYNF0VdAajP_5pYG-jG2JRrG72s)

Here's the rephrased content:

# HTML Editors

---

Getting Started with HTML

You don't need a professional editor to learn HTML. A simple text editor will suffice.

---

start

## Learn HTML Using Notepad or TextEdit

To create and modify web pages, you can use professional HTML editors. However, for learning purposes, we recommend using a simple text editor like Notepad (for PC) or TextEdit (for Mac).

Using a simple text editor is an effective way to learn the basics of HTML.

Follow these steps to create your first web page with Notepad or TextEdit:

stop

---

start

## Step 1: Open Notepad (PC)

For Windows 8 and later, open the Start Screen and type "Notepad". For Windows 7 and earlier, navigate to Start > Programs > Accessories > Notepad.

---

## Step 1: Open TextEdit (Mac)

On a Mac, open Finder > Applications > TextEdit. Additionally, adjust some preferences to ensure that files are saved correctly:

* Go to Preferences > Format and select "Plain Text".
* Under "Open and Save", check the box that says "Display HTML files as HTML code instead of formatted text".

Then, create a new document to enter your code.

stop

---

start

## Step 2: Write Some HTML

In Notepad or TextEdit, write or copy the following HTML code:

[insert code here]

stop

---

MidContent
Advertisement

---

start

## Step 3: Save the HTML Page

Save the file on your computer. In the menu bar, navigate to File > Save As.

Name the file "index.htm" and set the encoding to UTF-8 (the preferred encoding for HTML files).

Tip: You can use either .htm or .html as a file extension - it's up to you.

stop

---

start

## Step 4: View the HTML Page in Your Browser

Open the saved HTML file in your favorite browser by double-clicking on the file or right-clicking and selecting "Open with".

The result will look similar to this:

[insert code here]

stop

---

start

## Online Editor - Try it Yourself

Our free online editor allows you to edit HTML code and view the results in real-time. It's perfect for testing code quickly, with features like color coding and the ability to save and share your work:

Click on the "Try it Yourself" button to see how it works.

stop

## Spaces

If you want to create your own website and save your code online, try our free website builder called [Spaces](/spaces/):

[Get Started Now](/spaces/ "Get Started With  Spaces")

![](https://www.example.com/how-spaces-works3.png)

---

start

# Basic HTML Examples --- This chapter provides a foundation for understanding basic HTML concepts. Don't worry if you haven't learned about certain tags yet - we'll start from the basics! --- ## Creating an HTML Document To begin, every HTML document requires a document type declaration: ``. The HTML document itself starts with `` and ends with ``, while the visible part is between `` and ``. --- ## Defining the Document Type The `` declaration represents the document type, guiding browsers to display web pages correctly. This declaration must appear only once, at the top of the page (before any HTML tags), and is not case sensitive. For HTML5, this declaration looks like: --- ## Structuring Your Content with Headings HTML headings are defined using the `

` to `

` tags. The most important heading uses `

`, while the least important uses `

`: ---

Advertisement

--- ## Writing HTML Paragraphs HTML paragraphs use the `

` tag to define a block of text. --- ## Creating Links with the `` Tag HTML links are defined using the `` tag, which requires an `href` attribute specifying the link's destination. You'll learn more about attributes in a later chapter: --- ## Adding Images to Your HTML Content HTML images use the `` tag and provide attributes for the source file (`src`), alternative text (`alt`), width, and height: --- ## Exploring HTML Source Code Ever wondered how web pages are created? To view an HTML page's source code: ### View HTML Source Code Click `Ctrl + U` or right-click on the page and select "View Page Source" to open a new tab containing the HTML source code. ### Inspect an HTML Element Right-click on an element (or blank area) and choose "Inspect" to see what elements are made up of. You can also edit the HTML or CSS in the Elements or Styles panel that opens.

# HTML Elements --- An HTML element is a fundamental building block of an HTML document, consisting of a start tag, content, and an end tag. --- start ## The Basics of HTML Elements The HTML element encompasses everything from the start tag to the end tag: Content goes here... Let's look at some examples:

My First Heading

My first paragraph.

` element, contain no content and are considered empty elements. Empty elements do not require an end tag. stop --- start ## Nesting HTML Elements HTML elements can be nested to create a hierarchical structure of elements within other elements. All HTML documents consist of nested HTML elements. The following example demonstrates four HTML elements (``, ``, `

`, and `

`): ### Example Breakdown The `` element serves as the root element, defining the entire HTML document. It has a start tag `` and an end tag ``. Within the `` element lies the `` element: The `` element defines the document's body. It has a start tag `` and an end tag ``. Further inside, there are two additional elements: `

--- start ## Don't Forget the End Tag! While some HTML elements might display correctly without an end tag, it's crucial to include them for accurate rendering. **Remember:** Forgetting an end tag can lead to unexpected results and errors. Make sure to always include the closing tag! stop --- start ## Empty HTML Elements Some HTML elements, like `
`, contain no content and are considered empty elements. These elements do not require a closing tag: stop --- start ## Case Sensitivity in HTML HTML tags are case-insensitive: `

` is equivalent to `

`. While the HTML standard doesn't strictly require lowercase tags, it's recommended to use lowercase for HTML documents. For stricter document types like XHTML, lowercase is required. We always use lowercase tag names: stop --- --- ## HTML Tag Reference The [tag reference](/tags/default.asp) provides additional information on these tags and their attributes. | Tag | Description | | --- | --- | | `` | Defines the root of an HTML document | | `` | Defines the document's body | | `

to

` | Defines HTML headings | For a complete list of all available HTML tags, visit our [HTML Tag Reference](/tags/default.asp). --- ## Video: HTML Elements [![Tutorial on YouTube](https://www..com/html/images/yt_logo_rgb_dark.png)](https://youtu.be/vIoO52MdZFE&list=PLP9IO4UYNF0VdAajP_5pYG-jG2JRrG72s)

# HTML Attributes --- HTML attributes provide additional information about HTML elements. --- start ## HTML Attributes * All HTML elements can have **attributes** that provide **extra details** * These attributes are always specified in the **opening tag** of an element * Attributes usually come in name/value pairs, like: **name="value"** stop --- start ## The href Attribute The `` tag defines a hyperlink. The `href` attribute specifies the URL of the page the link points to: You'll learn more about links in our [HTML Links chapter](#). stop --- start ## The src Attribute The `` tag is used to embed an image in an HTML page. The `src` attribute specifies the path to the image to be displayed: There are two ways to specify the URL in the `src` attribute: **1. Absolute URL**: Links to an external image hosted on another website. Example: src="https://www.example.com/images/img_girl.jpg". **Important notes**: External images may be under copyright, and using them without permission could result in legal issues. Additionally, you have no control over external images, which can suddenly be removed or changed. **2. Relative URL**: Links to an image hosted within the website. The URL does not include the domain name. If the URL begins without a slash, it will be relative to the current page. Example: src="img_girl.jpg". If the URL begins with a slash, it will be relative to the domain. Example: src="/images/img_girl.jpg". **Tip**: It's generally best to use relative URLs, as they won't break if you change your domain. stop --- start ## The width and height Attributes The `` tag should also contain the `width` and `height` attributes, which specify the width and height of the image (in pixels): stop --- start ## The alt Attribute The required `alt` attribute for the `` tag specifies an alternate text for an image, in case it cannot be displayed. This could happen due to a slow connection, an error in the `src` attribute, or if the user uses a screen reader: You'll learn more about images in our [HTML Images chapter](#). stop --- start ## The style Attribute The `style` attribute is used to add styles to an element, such as color, font, size, and more. You'll learn more about styles in our [HTML Styles chapter](#). stop --- start ## The lang Attribute It's recommended to include the `lang` attribute inside the `` tag, declaring the language of your Web page. This helps search engines and browsers: The following example specifies English as the language: You can find all the language codes in our [HTML Language Code Reference](#). stop --- start ## The title Attribute The `title` attribute defines some extra information about an element. The value of the title attribute will be displayed as a tooltip when you mouse over the element: stop --- ## Recommendation: Use Lowercase Attributes While the HTML standard does not require lowercase attribute names, it's recommended to use them for consistency and better readability. This is especially important in stricter document types like XHTML. We always use lowercase attribute names at [our website](#). --- ## Recommendation: Quote Attribute Values The HTML standard does not require quotes around attribute values, but it's recommended to use them for clarity and better readability. Sometimes you may need to use quotes, as shown in this example: We always use quotes around attribute values at [our website](#). --- start ## Single or Double Quotes? Double quotes are the most common choice for attribute values, but single quotes can also be used. In some cases, when the attribute value contains double quotes, you may need to use single quotes: Or vice versa: stop --- start ## Chapter Summary * All HTML elements can have **attributes** that provide extra details * The `href` attribute of `` specifies the URL of the page the link points to * The `src` attribute of `` specifies the path to the image to be displayed * The `width` and `height` attributes of `` provide size information for images * The `alt` attribute of `` provides an alternate text for an image * The `style` attribute is used to add styles to an element, such as color, font, size, and more * The `lang` attribute of the `` tag declares the language of the Web page

# HTML Headings --- HTML headings are crucial for organizing and structuring content on a webpage. They serve as titles or subtitles that provide context and help readers navigate through your content. --- --- start ## What Are HTML Headings? HTML headings are defined using the `

` to `

` tags, with each tag representing a different level of importance. `

` is used for main headings, while `

`, `

`, and so on, are used for subheadings. Browsers automatically add some white space (a margin) before and after a heading. stop --- start ## Why Are Headings Important? Search engines rely on headings to understand the structure and content of your web pages. Additionally, users often skim through a page by its headings, making it essential to use them effectively. It's recommended to use `

` headings for main headings, followed by `

` headings, then less important `

`, and so on. **Note:** Use HTML headings solely for headings, avoiding the temptation to make text **BIG** or **bold** through heading tags. stop --- start ## Customizing Headings Each HTML heading has a default size. However, you can modify the size of any heading using the `style` attribute and the CSS `font-size` property: stop ---

# HTML Paragraphs --- HTML paragraphs always start on a new line and typically consist of a block of text. The purpose of a paragraph is to group related sentences together for better readability. ## Creating HTML Paragraphs The `

` element defines an HTML paragraph. Each paragraph has automatic white space (a margin) added before and after it by the browser, which helps to separate paragraphs from each other. ## Understanding HTML Display When creating HTML content, it's important to remember that how your code is displayed can vary depending on factors such as screen size, window resizing, or different devices. You cannot control the display of your content by adding extra spaces or line breaks in your HTML code. The browser will automatically remove any unnecessary white space and line breaks when displaying the page. ```

``` ## HTML Horizontal Rules The `
` element defines a thematic break in an HTML page, often displayed as a horizontal rule. This tag is used to separate content or define a change in your page: The `
` element is an empty tag and does not require a closing tag. ## Line Breaks Use the `
` element when you need a line break (new line) without starting a new paragraph: Like the `
` element, `
` is also an empty tag with no closing tag. ## The Poem Problem This poem will display on a single line due to its lack of line breaks and paragraphs: ## Solution - Using the HTML 
 Element

The `
` element defines preformatted text. Text inside a `
` element is displayed in a fixed-width font (usually Courier) and preserves both spaces and line breaks:

Here is the rephrased content in plain Markdown text: # HTML Styles --- Adding styles to your HTML elements is a great way to customize their appearance. The `style` attribute allows you to define various style properties, such as color, font, size, and more. --- --- start ## Styling with the Style Attribute You can apply styles to an HTML element using the `style` attribute. This attribute takes a CSS property-value pair as its value. The syntax for the `style` attribute is: ``` property: value ``` You'll learn more about CSS later in this tutorial, but for now, let's explore some basic styling options. stop --- start ## Background Color Use the `background-color` property to set the background color of an HTML element. This is a great way to add visual interest and differentiate elements from one another. stop ---

Advertisement

--- start ## Text Color The `color` property allows you to define the text color for an HTML element. This can be used to create visually appealing combinations of background and foreground colors. stop --- start ## Fonts Use the `font-family` property to specify the font used by an HTML element. You can choose from a wide range of fonts to create a unique look and feel. stop --- start ## Text Size The `font-size` property enables you to set the size of the text in an HTML element. This allows you to control the readability and visual appeal of your content. stop --- start ## Text Alignment Use the `text-align` property to define the horizontal alignment of text within an HTML element. This can be used to create visually appealing layouts and add emphasis to certain sections of text. stop --- start ## Summary * Use the `style` attribute to apply styles to your HTML elements * The `background-color` property sets the background color * The `color` property defines the text color * The `font-family` property specifies the font used * The `font-size` property sets the text size * The `text-align` property defines the horizontal text alignment stop --- --- ## Video: HTML Styles [![Tutorial on YouTube](https://www.example.com/html/images/yt_logo_rgb_dark.png)](https://youtu.be/twdNPJfbj_8&list=PLP9IO4UYNF0VdAajP_5pYG-jG2JRrG72s)

# Text Formatting in HTML

HTML provides various elements to format text with special meaning.

## HTML Formatting Elements

HTML offers several formatting elements that define specific types of text:

* `<bold>` - Bold text
* `<important>` - Important text
* `<italic>` - Italic text
* `<emphasized>` - Emphasized text
* `<highlighted>` - Highlighted text
* `<smaller>` - Smaller text
* `<deleted>` - Deleted text
* `<inserted>` - Inserted text
* `<subscript>` - Subscript text
* `<superscript>` - Superscript text

## HTML `<bold>` and `<important>` Elements

The HTML `<bold>` element defines bold text, without any additional emphasis.

The HTML `<important>` element defines important text, which is typically displayed in bold. The content inside this tag has strong importance and may be pronounced differently by a screen reader.

## HTML `<italic>` and `<emphasized>` Elements

The HTML `<italic>` element defines a part of text in an alternate voice or mood. This text is usually displayed in italic.

**Tip:** Use the `<i>` tag to indicate technical terms, phrases from other languages, thoughts, ship names, or any other special context.

The HTML `<emphasized>` element defines emphasized text, which is typically displayed in italic. A screen reader will pronounce the words inside this tag with an emphasis, using verbal stress.

## HTML `<small>` Element

The HTML `<small>` element defines smaller text:

## HTML `<highlighted>` Element

The HTML `<highlighted>` element defines text that should be marked or highlighted:

## HTML `<deleted>` Element

The HTML `<deleted>` element defines text that has been deleted from a document. Browsers usually strike a line through deleted text.

## HTML `<inserted>` Element

The HTML `<inserted>` element defines a text that has been inserted into a document. Browsers usually underline inserted text:

## HTML `<subscript>` and `<superscript>` Elements

The HTML `<subscript>` element defines subscript text, which appears half a character below the normal line. This type of text is often used for chemical formulas or mathematical equations.

The HTML `<superscript>` element defines superscript text, which appears half a character above the normal line. This type of text is often used for footnotes or citations.

## HTML Text Formatting Elements

| Tag | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `<bold>` | Defines bold text |
| `<emphasized>` | Defines emphasized text |
| `<italic>` | Defines a part of text in an alternate voice or mood |
| `<small>` | Defines smaller text |
| `<important>` | Defines important text |
| `<subscript>` | Defines subscripted text |
| `<superscript>` | Defines superscripted text |
| `<highlighted>` | Defines marked/highlighted text |
| `<deleted>` | Defines deleted text |
| `<inserted>` | Defines inserted text |

For a complete list of all available HTML tags, visit our [HTML Tag Reference](/tags/default.asp).

**Video:** Learn more about HTML formatting in this tutorial video: https://youtu.be/7FqQLqNIEY8&list=PLP9IO4UYNF0VdAajP_5pYG-jG2JRrG72s

# HTML Quotation and Citation Elements

---

This chapter covers the essential HTML elements for quotations, citations, abbreviations, and contact information.

## HTML Blockquote for Quotations

The `<blockquote>` element defines a block of quoted text from another source. Browsers typically indent this element to distinguish it from the surrounding content.

## HTML Quote for Short Quotations

Use the `<q>` tag to define a short quotation that can be embedded within a paragraph. The browser will automatically add quotation marks around the quoted text.

MidContent
Advertisement

## HTML Abbreviation for Abbreviations and Acronyms

The `<abbr>` element defines an abbreviation or acronym, such as "HTML", "CSS", or "ASAP". This element provides useful information to assistive technologies, search engines, and translation systems. To provide additional context, use the global title attribute to display a tooltip with the definition when hovering over the abbreviation.

## HTML Address for Contact Information

The `<address>` element defines contact information for the author or owner of a document, such as an email address, URL, physical address, phone number, or social media handle. This element typically renders in italics and includes line breaks before and after its contents.

## HTML Citation for Work Titles

Use the `<cite>` tag to define the title of a creative work, such as a book, poem, song, movie, painting, or sculpture. Note that a person's name is not considered the title of a work. The text within this element usually renders in italics.

## HTML Bi-Directional Override

The `<bdo>` element, which stands for Bi-Directional Override, allows you to override the current text direction. This element is useful when working with languages that are written from right to left or left to right.

---

Test Your Knowledge with Exercises!

| Tag | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `<abbr>` | Defines an abbreviation or acronym |
| `<address>` | Defines contact information for the author/owner of a document |
| `<bdo>` | Defines the text direction |
| `<blockquote>` | Defines a section that is quoted from another source |
| `<cite>` | Defines the title of a work |
| `<q>` | Defines a short inline quotation |

For a comprehensive list of all available HTML tags, visit our [HTML Tag Reference].
